Party_Details.Name: Falmac Limited

Facts.Text: Falmac Limited was a Catalist Sponsor-supervised company at the time it was de-listed by the Singapore Exchange Securities Trading Ltd on 2011-08-22.

On 2004-10-27, Falmac Limited issued 44,975,120 ordinary shares of $0.01 each at par for cash for an aggregate cash consideration of $1,700,000 to reduce amounts owing to creditor banks and to pay professional fees related to a restructuring.

On 2004-10-27, Falmac Limited issued 34,448,000 ordinary shares of $0.01 each at an issue price of $0.311 per share as settlement for partial outstanding loans of $10,713,430 owing by Falmac Limited and its group to creditor banks.

On 2004-10-27, creditor banks holding conversion shares in Falmac Limited undertook not to dispose of any conversion shares for six months and not to dispose of more than 25% of their conversion shares for 12 months from that date.
As of 2004-10-27, debts owing by Falmac Limited and its group to creditor banks amounting to approximately $10.7 million were capitalised into new ordinary shares in Falmac Limited.

As of 2005-12-31, Falmac Limited had a remaining balance of about $4.1 million and $3.9 million owing to creditor banks, to be repaid by disposal of assets in Singapore and by restructuring $3.9 million into a restructured loan repayable by 12 equal quarterly instalments over three years.

On 2006-02-20, creditor banks demanded repayment from Falmac Limited of the entire restructured loan together with interest amounting to about $3.97 million after Falmac Limited defaulted on three repayment instalments and had a shortfall of $250,000 from planned asset sales.
Falmac Limited's financial statements for the year ended 2006-12-31 disclosed defaults in payments under certain indebtedness agreements.
Falmac Limited's financial statements for the year ended 2007-12-31 disclosed defaults in payments under certain indebtedness agreements.
Auditors' notes to Falmac Limited's financial statements dated 2005-12-31 recorded that the company was renegotiating with creditor banks for a revised bank borrowings repayment scheme.

As of 2005-12-31 and in subsequent years, the current liabilities of Falmac Limited and its subsidiaries exceeded their current assets, and auditors in Singapore expressed difficulty in supporting a going concern basis for the company and its subsidiaries.

By 2008-12-31, Falmac Limited's business operations were mainly carried out in Tianjin, People’s Republic of China, through two subsidiaries, Falmac Machinery (Tianjin) Ltd and Falmac Textile (Tianjin) Co Ltd, each owning a factory in Tianjin.
As of 2008-12-31, Falmac Limited and its Tianjin subsidiaries had significant liabilities and were described as debt-ridden and loss-making businesses.

After 2008-12-31, creditor banks assigned all their interests and rights in relation to Falmac Limited's bank loans to Business Corporate Services Pte Ltd, making Business Corporate Services Pte Ltd a 16.5% shareholder and a substantial creditor with an outstanding loan of approximately S$4.5 million.
On 2009-03-09, Falmac Limited announced the execution of a contract dated 2009-03-08 with CNMC Goldmine Limited to acquire all shares in CNMC Goldmine Limited.
On 2009-12-22, Falmac Limited applied to the Singapore Exchange Securities Trading Ltd for an extension of time until 2011-04-30 to submit a resumption proposal for trading of its shares.
On 2009-12-28, Falmac Limited announced that it intended to divest its shareholding in its Tianjin subsidiaries and to write off the investment costs of the Tianjin subsidiaries.
On 2010-05-21, Falmac Limited signed a memorandum of understanding with Unitex Global Limited for the disposal of Falmac Limited's shareholding in its Tianjin subsidiaries.

On 2011-07-11, Falmac Limited entered into a sale and purchase agreement with Unitex Global Limited to dispose of 100% equity interest in its Tianjin subsidiaries for an aggregate consideration of S$2, with effectiveness stated as 2011-07-11 conditions including shareholders' approval, completion of acquisition of CNMC Goldmine Limited, and resumption of trading of Falmac Limited's shares.

On 2010-12-24, Falmac Limited announced that it and CNMC Goldmine Limited agreed to extend the long-stop date under their contract from 2010-12-31 to 2011-03-31 with an option to extend further to 2011-06-30.

On 2011-04-28, Falmac Limited submitted a resumption proposal to the Singapore Exchange Securities Trading Ltd in relation to its contract to acquire 100% of the share capital of CNMC Goldmine Limited.

On 2009-12-14, the new board of Falmac Limited held a meeting at which the chairman informed the board that the Tianjin subsidiaries had defaulted on loans to Avenue Asia Mauritius Ltd and Agricultural Bank of China and proposed rationalising the textile businesses, including disposal of loss-making subsidiaries in China.

As at 2008-12-31, Falmac Machinery (Tianjin) Ltd owed approximately RMB12.1 million to Avenue Asia Mauritius Limited, which had obtained a judgment and begun selling assets including two staff apartments in Tianjin.
As at 2008-12-31, Falmac Textile (Tianjin) Co Ltd owed approximately RMB19.2 million to Agricultural Bank of China, and its factory was locked up by Agricultural Bank of China.

Auditors' notes to Falmac Limited's financial statements dated 2005-12-31 recorded that a core part of the restructuring plan was a debt conversion to reduce current liabilities and immediate cash requirements.

On 2009-08-17, an article about Falmac Limited and its former chief executive officer appeared in The Edge Singapore magazine, and on the same day a trading halt was called on Falmac Limited's shares.
On 2009-08-18, the Singapore Exchange Securities Trading Ltd requested Falmac Limited to clarify statements in the article published in The Edge Singapore magazine.
On 2009-08-19, Falmac Limited's former chief executive officer sent a response to the Singapore Exchange Securities Trading Ltd regarding the article in The Edge Singapore magazine.
On 2009-08-21, trading of Falmac Limited's shares was suspended after the three-day trading halt period.

On 2010-01-07, the new board of Falmac Limited sent a clarification to the Singapore Exchange Securities Trading Ltd regarding matters reported in The Edge Singapore article, after which the exchange did not raise further queries.
On 2010-03-20, Falmac Limited published a notice in The Straits Times stating that the appointment of Leon Zhao as legal representative of its Tianjin subsidiaries was unauthorised.

On 2009-12-01, Falmac Limited, as 100% shareholder of its Tianjin subsidiaries, passed two shareholder resolutions removing Leon Zhao as legal representative of the Tianjin subsidiaries and appointing Pua Yeow Seng as legal representative.

On 2010-10-29, Falmac Limited commenced legal proceedings in China against its former chief executive officer and Leon Zhao seeking to invalidate the change of legal representative of the Tianjin subsidiaries.
On 2010-11-05, the Tianjin subsidiaries filed civil indictments in China against Leon Zhao seeking the return of company seals, business licences and other documents.
On 2010-12-03, Falmac Limited and its Tianjin subsidiaries withdrew the legal proceedings in China against its former chief executive officer and Leon Zhao.
On 2009-03-20, Falmac Limited's new board caused a Singapore Exchange announcement stating that the appointment of Leon Zhao as legal representative of the Tianjin subsidiaries was unauthorised.

On 2009-10-20, Choo and Kuan, as representatives of Falmac Limited, travelled to Tianjin to visit the factories of the Tianjin subsidiaries and to obtain financial records for preparation of Falmac Limited's consolidated financial statements.

On 2009-10-20, Choo and Kuan were not allowed to enter the Falmac Textile (Tianjin) Co Ltd factory by a security guard at the entrance and then proceeded to visit the Falmac Machinery (Tianjin) Ltd factory.

On 2009-07-09, the former board of Falmac Limited passed a resolution to transfer the entire equity in the Tianjin subsidiaries to Sino Vision (HK) Ltd by way of converting debts for shares in the Tianjin subsidiaries.

On 2009-07-30, Falmac Limited entered into two Equity Transfer Agreements with Sino Vision (HK) Ltd to transfer Falmac Limited's shares in each of the Tianjin subsidiaries to Sino Vision (HK) Ltd, with Article 7 providing that formalities to effect the transfer, including corporate approvals and registration, were to be completed within 12 months.
On 2009-07-30, the Equity Transfer Agreements between Falmac Limited and Sino Vision (HK) Ltd were signed on behalf of Falmac Limited by independent director Yu Wei Ying.

On 2011-08-18, Falmac Limited's Chinese counsel inspected records at the Commercial and Industrial Bureau of Tianjin and observed entries showing Sino Vision (HK) Ltd as shareholder of the Tianjin subsidiaries with a registration date of 2011-08-18.
On 2009-12-14, the new board of Falmac Limited passed a resolution to divest the Tianjin subsidiaries, shareholders' approval.

On 2011-07-11, Falmac Limited issued a Singapore Exchange announcement stating that Avenue Asia Mauritius Limited had obtained judgment against Falmac Machinery (Tianjin) Ltd and that Agricultural Bank of China had locked up the factory of Falmac Textile (Tianjin) Co Ltd.
On 2009-03-20, Falmac Limited announced that its board intended to divest all ex-Singapore businesses as part of a restructuring plan associated with a proposed reverse takeover.
On 2011-08-22, the Singapore Exchange Securities Trading Ltd de-listed Falmac Limited from Catalist.
On 2009-10-30, Falmac Limited filed a Writ of Summons commencing Suit No 935 of 2009 against five individuals who had constituted its board of directors during the relevant period.

On 2010-09-08, Falmac Limited amended its Statement of Claim to include allegations that its former chief executive officer had changed the legal representative of the Tianjin subsidiaries to Leon Zhao with effect from 2009-09-01.

On 2011-11-21, Falmac Limited filed Statement of Claim (Amendment No 2) introducing allegations that its former chief executive officer had caused the transfer of Falmac Limited's shares in the Tianjin subsidiaries to Sino Vision (HK) Ltd without shareholders' approval.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether the former chief executive officer and director caused Falmac Limited to lose control or ownership of its Tianjin subsidiaries and contributed to the suspension and eventual de-listing of Falmac Limited through pre-dispute conduct, including corporate decisions on legal representation, equity transfers, and financial reporting.
Party_Details.Rule: Directors of a Singapore-incorporated and SGX-listed company are act in good faith in the interests of the company and to exercise reasonable diligence, and must obtain shareholders' approval before carrying into effect proposals to dispose of the whole or substantially the whole of the company's undertaking or property.
Party_Details.Application: Falmac Limited's pre-dispute records show persistent financial distress, restructuring of bank debts into equity, and reliance on external investors for a proposed reverse takeover. The board approved a resolution on 2009-07-09 to transfer the equity in the Tianjin subsidiaries to Sino Vision (HK) Ltd and executed conditional Equity Transfer Agreements on 2009-07-30, with formalities including shareholder approval and registration to be completed within 12 months. On 2009-09-01, the legal representative of the Tianjin subsidiaries was changed from the former chief executive officer to Leon Zhao with board-level approvals in the Tianjin subsidiaries and letters of appointment from Falmac Limited. After 2009-10-20, Falmac Limited's new board treated the Tianjin subsidiaries as assets for divestment, passed a resolution on 2009-12-14 to divest them, and later entered into a sale and purchase agreement with Unitex Global Limited on 2011-07-11 for S$2, while also pursuing a reverse takeover involving CNMC Goldmine Limited and submitting a resumption proposal on 2011-04-28. During this period, the Singapore Exchange maintained a suspension of trading from 2009-08-21 and ultimately de-listed Falmac Limited on 2011-08-22 after rejecting the resumption proposal. These pre-dispute events define the factual context for assessing whether the former chief executive officer's conduct regarding the Tianjin subsidiaries and corporate reporting contributed to the loss of those subsidiaries and to the company's inability to maintain its listing.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log:
The court analysed Falmac Limited's claims against its former chief executive officer and director (D2) across several heads, focusing on the alleged loss of the Tianjin subsidiaries and the suspension and de-listing of Falmac. On the 'unauthorised legal representative' argument, the court held that Falmac had not proved that the appointment of Leon Zhao as legal representative of the Tianjin subsidiaries was unlawful or that Leon Zhao was D2's nominee used to retain de facto control. The appointment was approved by the boards of the Tianjin subsidiaries and by Falmac through letters of appointment signed by D2, and was recognised by the Commerce Commission of Ninghe. The court emphasised that allegations of dishonesty must be distinctly pleaded and proved; Falmac's pleadings and evidence did not meet this standard, and dishonesty was not squarely put to D2 in cross-examination. The court also found Falmac's evidence on denial of access to the Tianjin factories weak. For the Falmac Textile factory, contemporaneous records showed that Agricultural Bank of China had locked up the factory due to loan defaults, breaking the causal chain to D2. For the Falmac Machinery factory, the court treated the evidence of former general manager Zhang with caution because he was remunerated by Falmac and engaged to assist in litigation; there was no contemporaneous protest or action by Falmac after the alleged 2009-10-20 incident, and the allegation only surfaced in amended pleadings in 2010. The court concluded that Falmac had not proved that D2 excluded its representatives from the factories or that this caused loss of effective control.

On the s 160 Companies Act complaint, the court accepted that the former board, including D2, passed a resolution on 2009-07-09 to transfer the equity in the Tianjin subsidiaries to Sino Vision (HK) Ltd and that Equity Transfer Agreements were signed on 2009-07-30. However, the court interpreted Article 7 of the Equity Transfer Agreements as making them conditional contracts requiring completion of formalities, including shareholder approval and registration, within 12 months. D2's evidence that shareholder approval was required and that he did not implement the transfer after his removal on 2009-10-05 was accepted. The court held that the mere execution of conditional agreements did not amount to 'carrying into effect' a disposal under s 160(1) without shareholder approval. The Basic Information documents from the Tianjin Commercial Bureau were not admitted as proof of their contents under the Evidence Act, though the Chinese lawyer's eye-witness account of seeing Sino Vision's name and a date of 2011-08-18 was accepted as to what he observed, not as proof of legal effect. The court inferred that any registration in 2011 would have required involvement of the new board, given the 12-month period in Article 7 and D2's departure in 2009.

The court further noted that the divestment of the Tianjin subsidiaries was part of a reverse takeover strategy initiated by Business Corporate Services Pte Ltd and Choo, who viewed Falmac as a shell for a backdoor listing of CNMC Goldmine Limited. Emails from early 2009 showed that Wong and Choo were driving the plan to dispose of the Tianjin subsidiaries for a nominal sum and restructure Falmac into a shell. After the new board took over on 2009-10-05, it passed a resolution on 2009-12-14 to divest the Tianjin subsidiaries, signed an MOU with Unitex on 2010-05-21, and executed the Unitex SPA on 2011-07-11 for S$2, treating the Tianjin subsidiaries as assets available for sale despite the alleged loss of control. The court found this inconsistent with Falmac's theory that D2 had already removed the subsidiaries from its reach.

Regarding the trading suspension and de-listing, the court accepted that the 2009-08-17 article in The Edge triggered a trading halt and subsequent suspension on 2009-08-21, and that SGX-ST required clarification. The new board's clarification on 2010-01-07 was accepted by SGX-ST, and there were no further regulatory queries on that issue. The court held that the continued suspension and eventual de-listing on 2011-08-22 were due to Falmac's inability to submit an acceptable resumption proposal under the Listing Rules, not to D2's earlier statements. Evidence from Wong showed that the delays related to the complexity of meeting SGX's requirements for mineral, oil and gas listings and the need for detailed geological and financial due diligence on CNMC Goldmine Limited. Wong conceded in cross-examination that the delays in resuming trading had nothing to do with D2. The court therefore rejected Falmac's claim that D2's conduct in relation to The Edge article caused the loss of Falmac's listed status.

On the 'secondary claims' concerning alleged non-compliance with AGM timing, financial statement preparation, and Listing Rules on directors and audit committees, the court distinguished between statutory or regulatory breaches and breaches of fiduciary duty. It held that failure to meet deadlines for AGMs or filings, without dishonesty or disloyalty, does not automatically constitute a breach of fiduciary duty. The delays in holding the 2008 AGM and finalising accounts were attributed to Falmac's financial distress, resignation of finance staff, the engagement of Bizcorp, and a hard disk failure. D2's refusal to sign the 2008 accounts without access to source documents was considered a reasonable stance under s 199 of the Companies Act. The AGM was eventually held on 2009-12-14, and Falmac did not prove any pecuniary loss from the delay. For Listing Rule breaches on independent directors and audit committee composition, the court found that Falmac had not identified a specific fiduciary obligation breached by D2. The resignations of independent directors on 2009-07-30 were driven by their own decisions and by Choo's earlier plan to replace them; D2 could not compel them to remain, and Falmac lacked resources to appoint replacements. The omission to state 'personal reasons' in SGX announcements was attributed to the company secretary. On the resident director issue, the court accepted D2's evidence that his employment pass renewal was refused by the Ministry of Manpower due to Falmac's CPF payment history, and that updating his address to a foreign address did not itself amount to a fiduciary breach.

Overall, the court concluded that Falmac had not established that D2 acted dishonestly or disloyally, or that his pre-dispute conduct caused the loss of the Tianjin subsidiaries or Falmac's de-listing. The main claims under the unauthorised legal representative argument and the s 160 complaint failed, as did the secondary claims. D2's counterclaim for unpaid salary and director's fees was considered separately. The High Court therefore dismissed Falmac Limited's claims against D2, and the Court of Appeal later dismissed the appeal in 2014.

Party_Details.Name: Cheng Ji Lai Charlie

Facts.Text: On 2004-10-27, Cheng Ji Lai Charlie was appointed a director of Falmac Limited.
On 2004-12-15, Cheng Ji Lai Charlie was appointed Chief Executive Officer of Falmac Limited.
According to his account, Cheng Ji Lai Charlie was introduced into Falmac Limited by director Fei Xue Jun in 2004 when Falmac Limited required capital injection and loan restructuring.
On 2009-07-20, Cheng Ji Lai Charlie resigned as Chief Executive Officer of Falmac Limited with immediate effect.
On 2009-10-05, Cheng Ji Lai Charlie was removed as a director of Falmac Limited at an Extraordinary General Meeting.

On 2009-09-01, Cheng Ji Lai Charlie ceased to be the legal representative of Falmac Machinery (Tianjin) Ltd and Falmac Textile (Tianjin) Co Ltd, and Leon Zhao was appointed as legal representative of both Tianjin subsidiaries.
On 2009-09-01, Falmac Limited issued letters of appointment, signed by Cheng Ji Lai Charlie, approving the appointment of Leon Zhao as legal representative of its Tianjin subsidiaries.
On 2009-10-13, the Commerce Commission of Ninghe approved the change of legal representative of the Tianjin subsidiaries from Cheng Ji Lai Charlie to Leon Zhao.

On 2009-07-09, the former board of Falmac Limited, including Cheng Ji Lai Charlie, passed a resolution to transfer the entire equity in the Tianjin subsidiaries to Sino Vision (HK) Ltd by way of converting debts for shares.

On 2009-07-30, Falmac Limited entered into two Equity Transfer Agreements with Sino Vision (HK) Ltd to transfer its shares in each Tianjin subsidiary, with Article 7 providing that formalities to effect the transfer, including corporate approvals and registration, were to be completed within 12 months.

On 2009-10-19, Cheng Ji Lai Charlie emailed Falmac Limited's external auditors stating that he had resigned in September 2009 as Chairman of the Board of Directors of both Tianjin subsidiaries and no longer held any appointment in those companies or in Falmac Limited.

On 2009-01-20, Peter Choo emailed Cheng Ji Lai Charlie stating that he was serious about Falmac Limited, had spent about S$80,000, intended to assume the role of Chairman of Falmac Limited, and urged Cheng Ji Lai Charlie to arrange for the three independent directors to resign quickly.

On 2009-01-28, Peter Choo emailed Cheng Ji Lai Charlie stating that more than S$100,000 had been spent, that he would soon pay for a bank settlement due on 2009-02-20, and listing issues to settle during an upcoming visit, including share placement, sale of ex-Singapore businesses, and announcement of a deal involving CNMC Goldmine Limited.

On 2009-02-26, Peter Choo emailed Cheng Ji Lai Charlie stating that CNMC Goldmine Limited gave Fei Xue Jun a chance to make money and that he planned to sign agreements with Cheng Ji Lai Charlie and Fei Xue Jun the following week.

On 2009-03-24, Peter Choo emailed Cheng Ji Lai Charlie stating that the Singapore Exchange had called to ask why he was not buying Falmac Limited and that he planned to sign a non-legal conditional memorandum of understanding with Falmac Limited to acquire CNMC Goldmine Limited.

On 2009-03-30, Peter Choo emailed Cheng Ji Lai Charlie stating that CNMC Goldmine Limited was progressing and that Wong Lock Chee had compiled agreements to enable the parties to move ahead with restructuring Falmac Limited.

On 2009-03-25, Cheng Ji Lai Charlie emailed Peter Choo stating that Tianjin Kanbo Machinery Ltd was interested in buying the Tianjin subsidiaries for S$1 and that Superway Logistics Limited would pay S$100,000 for half of six banks' loans to Falmac Limited, and requested that this information be passed to Wong Lock Chee.

On 2009-03-30, Wong Lock Chee of Business Corporate Services Pte Ltd emailed Cheng Ji Lai Charlie five draft agreements, including a memorandum of understanding between Falmac Limited and CNMC Goldmine Limited, draft settlement agreements with Cheng Ji Lai Charlie and Fei Xue Jun, and draft agreements involving Tianjin Kanbo Machinery Ltd and Superway Logistics Limited.

In February 2009, Business Corporate Services Pte Ltd and Peter Choo engaged Bizcorp Management Pte Ltd to take over Falmac Limited's financial reporting functions after the resignation of Falmac Limited's finance personnel in late 2008.

By 2009-07-31, Cheng Ji Lai Charlie had refused to sign off on the 2008 management accounts prepared by Bizcorp Management Pte Ltd, and the Annual General Meeting for the year ended 2008-12-31 could not be held by the extended deadline.

On 2009-08-05, Business Corporate Services Pte Ltd requisitioned an Extraordinary General Meeting of Falmac Limited to remove Cheng Ji Lai Charlie and Fei Xue Jun as directors and appoint a new board.

On 2009-09-04, Cheng Ji Lai Charlie and Fei Xue Jun passed a board resolution appointing Bih Li & Lee to apply for an injunction on behalf of Falmac Limited to stop the convening of the Extraordinary General Meeting.
On 2009-09-04, Cheng Ji Lai Charlie and Fei Xue Jun caused Falmac Limited to seek an injunction to postpone the Extraordinary General Meeting, resulting in a one-week postponement to 2009-10-05.
On 2009-08-17, statements attributed to Cheng Ji Lai Charlie about accounting irregularities and false accounting at Falmac Limited appeared in an article in The Edge Singapore magazine.
On 2009-08-23, Cheng Ji Lai Charlie sent a draft announcement to the Singapore Exchange Securities Trading Ltd to clarify his comments reported in The Edge Singapore article.
On 2009-07-22, Cheng Ji Lai Charlie informed Falmac Limited's company secretary that Avenue Asia Mauritius Ltd had obtained a judgment against Falmac Machinery (Tianjin) Ltd in June 2009.
On 2009-07-26, Cheng Ji Lai Charlie provided further information to the company secretary about the judgment obtained by Avenue Asia Mauritius Ltd against Falmac Machinery (Tianjin) Ltd.

By 2009-07-31, the Ministry of Manpower had refused to renew Cheng Ji Lai Charlie's employment pass, and he updated his address with the Accounting and Corporate Regulatory Authority to a foreign address.
On 2011-06-14, Cheng Ji Lai Charlie attended a trade exhibition in Shanghai where the Tianjin subsidiaries had a booth, and he met former colleagues there.
On 2009-10-29, Falmac Limited's new board sent a letter to Cheng Ji Lai Charlie at his Shanghai residential address informing him of his removal as legal representative of the Tianjin subsidiaries.
From late 2009 to August 2011, Falmac Limited employed former Tianjin Machinery general manager Zhang Shuren as a representative in China to assist with lawsuits involving the Tianjin subsidiaries.
By July 2009, Cheng Ji Lai Charlie had outstanding claims for unpaid salary as Chief Executive Officer of Falmac Limited and unpaid director's fees, which later formed the basis of his counterclaim.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, based on pre-dispute conduct, Cheng Ji Lai Charlie as director and chief executive officer implemented or caused transactions and corporate actions that resulted in Falmac Limited losing control or ownership of its Tianjin subsidiaries and contributed to the suspension and de-listing of Falmac Limited.
Party_Details.Rule: A director and chief executive officer of a Singapore-incorporated and SGX-listed company is act in good faith in the interests of the company, exercise reasonable diligence, and ensure that proposals to dispose of the whole or substantially the whole of the company's undertaking or property are carried into effect only after approval by the company in general meeting.
Party_Details.Application: The pre-dispute record shows that Cheng Ji Lai Charlie joined Falmac Limited's board in 2004 and became chief executive officer in 2004-12, during a period of financial distress requiring capital injections and debt restructuring. In 2009, he engaged with Business Corporate Services Pte Ltd and Peter Choo on a reverse takeover strategy that contemplated restructuring Falmac Limited into a shell and divesting the loss-making Tianjin subsidiaries. On 2009-07-09, the former board, including him, passed a resolution to transfer the equity in the Tianjin subsidiaries to Sino Vision (HK) Ltd via a debt-for-share arrangement, and on 2009-07-30, Falmac Limited executed conditional Equity Transfer Agreements with Sino Vision (HK) Ltd that required completion of formalities, including corporate approvals and registration, within 12 months. On 2009-09-01, he signed letters approving the appointment of Leon Zhao as legal representative of the Tianjin subsidiaries, and the change was later approved by the Commerce Commission of Ninghe. He resigned as chief executive officer on 2009-07-20 and was removed as director on 2009-10-05, after which the new board pursued its own divestment plan, passed a resolution on 2009-12-14 to divest the Tianjin subsidiaries, and entered into a sale and purchase agreement with Unitex Global Limited on 2011-07-11 for S$2. During this period, he communicated with auditors about his resignation from roles in the Tianjin subsidiaries and with the Singapore Exchange about statements reported in The Edge Singapore article. The Singapore Exchange maintained a trading suspension from 2009-08-21 and later de-listed Falmac Limited on 2011-08-22 after rejecting a resumption proposal submitted by the new board on 2011-04-28. These pre-dispute facts Cheng Ji Lai Charlie participated in board-level decisions and documentation concerning the Tianjin subsidiaries and in communications with investors and regulators, while the implementation of later divestment steps and the resumption proposal occurred under the new board after his removal.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log:
The court examined whether Cheng Ji Lai Charlie (D2) was responsible for Falmac Limited's alleged loss of the Tianjin subsidiaries and for the suspension and de-listing of Falmac's shares. On the allegation that he unlawfully changed the legal representative of the Tianjin subsidiaries to his nominee, the court found that the appointment of Leon Zhao as legal representative on 2009-09-01 was approved by the boards of the Tianjin subsidiaries and by Falmac through letters of appointment signed by D2, and was recognised by the Commerce Commission of Ninghe. The court held that Falmac had not proved that the appointment was unlawful or that Leon Zhao was D2's nominee used to conceal D2's continued control. Allegations of dishonesty were not distinctly pleaded or put to D2 in cross-examination, and the court reiterated that dishonesty must be specifically alleged and proved with cogent evidence. The court also noted that if D2 had intended to retain control, there was no clear reason for him to relinquish the legal representative role while remaining a board member of the Tianjin subsidiaries and allegedly retaining company seals.

On the claim that D2 denied Falmac access to the Tianjin factories, the court distinguished between the two subsidiaries. For Falmac Textile (Tianjin) Co Ltd, contemporaneous board minutes and SGX announcements showed that Agricultural Bank of China had locked up the factory due to loan defaults, and the court concluded that any inability to access that factory was attributable to the bank's enforcement, not to D2. For Falmac Machinery (Tianjin) Ltd, the court treated the evidence of former general manager Zhang with caution because Zhang was employed and paid by Falmac to assist with litigation and admitted he was engaged to testify. The court found it unsafe to rely solely on his account that D2 telephoned him on 2009-10-20 to expel Falmac's representatives, especially since Falmac did not contemporaneously protest or act on the alleged incident and only raised it in amended pleadings in 2010. The court also observed that Falmac did not file police reports in China alleging theft or hijacking of the factories, and that civil proceedings in China against D2 and Leon Zhao were withdrawn without service on D2, partly because Falmac had not provided his Shanghai address to its Chinese lawyers.

Regarding the s 160 Companies Act complaint, the court accepted that the former board, including D2, passed a resolution on 2009-07-09 to transfer the equity in the Tianjin subsidiaries to Sino Vision (HK) Ltd and that Equity Transfer Agreements were signed on 2009-07-30. However, the court interpreted Article 7 of the Equity Transfer Agreements as making them conditional contracts requiring completion of formalities, including shareholder approval and registration, within 12 months. D2's evidence that shareholder approval was required and that he did not implement the transfer after his removal on 2009-10-05 was accepted. The court held that executing conditional agreements did not amount to 'carrying into effect' a disposal under s 160(1) without shareholder approval. The Basic Information documents from the Tianjin Commercial Bureau were not admitted as proof of their contents under the Evidence Act, though the Chinese lawyer's testimony that he saw Sino Vision's name and a date of 2011-08-18 was accepted as to what he observed. The court inferred that any registration in 2011 would have required involvement of the new board, given the 12-month period in Article 7 and D2's departure in 2009. Falmac did not allege that the 2009 board resolution was fraudulent or invalid, nor did it plead that D2 had an interest in Sino Vision or continued to control the Tianjin subsidiaries through Sino Vision and Leon Zhao.

On the trading suspension and de-listing, the court accepted that statements attributed to D2 in The Edge Singapore article on 2009-08-17 triggered a trading halt and subsequent suspension on 2009-08-21, and that SGX-ST required clarification. The new board's clarification on 2010-01-07 was accepted by SGX-ST, and there were no further regulatory queries on that issue. The court held that the continued suspension and eventual de-listing on 2011-08-22 were due to Falmac's inability to submit an acceptable resumption proposal under Rule 1304 and related Listing Rules, not to D2's earlier statements. Evidence from Wong showed that the delays related to the complexity of meeting SGX's requirements for mineral, oil and gas listings and the need for detailed geological and financial due diligence on CNMC Goldmine Limited. Wong conceded that the delays in resuming trading had nothing to do with D2. The court therefore rejected Falmac's claim that D2's conduct in relation to The Edge article caused the loss of Falmac's listed status.

On the secondary claims, the court held that non-compliance with statutory deadlines for AGMs and financial statements, or with Listing Rules on independent directors and audit committees, does not automatically amount to a breach of fiduciary duty. The delays in holding the 2008 AGM and finalising accounts were attributed to Falmac's financial distress, resignation of finance staff, the engagement of Bizcorp, and a hard disk failure. D2's refusal to sign the 2008 accounts without access to source documents was considered reasonable under s 199 of the Companies Act. The AGM was eventually held on 2009-12-14, and Falmac did not prove any pecuniary loss from the delay. For Listing Rule breaches on independent directors and audit committee composition, the court found that Falmac had not identified a specific fiduciary obligation breached by D2. The resignations of independent directors on 2009-07-30 were driven by their own decisions and by Choo's earlier plan to replace them; D2 could not compel them to remain, and Falmac lacked resources to appoint replacements. The omission to state 'personal reasons' in SGX announcements was attributed to the company secretary. On the resident director issue, the court accepted D2's evidence that his employment pass renewal was refused by the Ministry of Manpower due to Falmac's CPF payment history, and that updating his address to a foreign address did not itself amount to a fiduciary breach.

The court concluded that Falmac had not established that D2 acted dishonestly or disloyally, or that his pre-dispute conduct caused the loss of the Tianjin subsidiaries or Falmac's de-listing. The main claims under the unauthorised legal representative argument and the s 160 complaint failed, as did the secondary claims. The High Court dismissed Falmac Limited's claims against D2 and addressed his counterclaim for unpaid salary and director's fees separately. The Court of Appeal later dismissed Falmac's appeal, thereby affirming the High Court's decision that D2 was not liable on the pleaded claims.
